title: How to AI-proof your job | FT #shorts

transcript.text: How to AI proof your job. If you've used
the latest agentic AI tools like clawed
code, you'll know we've crossed a
threshold. Building functioning software
or analyzing complex data has gone
almost overnight from a scarce
specialist skill to something routine
and ubiquitous. Yesterday's blacksmiths,
so the argument goes, are today's web
developers and data scientists. But I'm
[snorts] not sure that's necessarily
true. For years, we've been told that
STEM and coding are the most valuable
skills for the 21st century economy. and
demand for these roles has indeed been
strong, but the evidence suggests it was
not necessarily a straightforward story
of the quantitative skills driving that
success. A landmark 2017 study by
Harvard economist David Demming found
that social skills more than
mathematical ones have seen the biggest
rewards in the labor market. I've
extended his analysis to the present day
and the conclusion still holds. The jobs
that have thrived aren't the purely
mathematical ones. They're the roles
combining quantitative ability with
skills like collaboration, persuasion,
and creative problem solving.
Consultants, economists, doctors, even
software developers themselves, whose
jobs involve much more creativity and
collaboration than is often appreciated.
Meanwhile, highly mathematical jobs with
less emphasis on working with others
have seen much more muted growth. This
is actually a reversal from a few
decades ago. In 1980, people strong with
numbers but weak on social skills
outearned those who had strong soft
skills but weaker quantitative
abilities. Today that's flipped. It's
interesting to consider all of this as
AI starts to automate coding. In many
ways, this finding could be reassuring.
The best coders and data scientists have
succeeded not simply because they can
write functions and formulas, but
because of the ideas, the teamwork, and
the creative problem solving they
brought to their coding. And if you stop
to think, was it ever really the act of
writing the code that was the fun part
of the job, or was it the things that
code enabled you to build and discover?
